cards.long_answer: If you parallelise or accelerate part of a system, your overall speedup is bounded by the part you didn't speed up. The slowest unaccelerated component caps the total improvement.
cards.short_answer: The slowest unaccelerated part caps your total speedup.
cards.hint: Think about serial vs parallel sections.

cards.prompt: What does the CAP theorem say?
cards.long_answer: In a distributed data store, you can guarantee at most two of: Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance. In practice, partitions happen, so the real choice is between consistency and availability during a partition.
cards.short_answer: Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance — pick two.

cards.prompt: What is the difference between latency and throughput?
cards.long_answer: Latency is the time to complete a single operation (seconds per request). Throughput is the rate of operations completed (requests per second). Adding parallelism can raise throughput without lowering latency.
cards.short_answer: Latency = time per op; throughput = ops per second.

cards.prompt: Why is read-after-write consistency hard in a replicated database?
cards.long_answer: After a write to the leader, replicas catch up asynchronously. A read routed to a lagging replica may not see the just-written value. Solutions: route reads to the leader, use synchronous replication, or use causal-consistency tokens.
cards.short_answer: Replicas lag the leader; lagging reads miss recent writes.

cards.prompt: What is a tail latency, and why does it matter?
cards.long_answer: Tail latency is the latency of the slowest few percent of requests (e.g. p99). In a service that fans out to many backends, the user-visible latency is dominated by the slowest backend, so even rare slow responses matter a lot.
cards.short_answer: Slow tail = slow user, especially with fan-out.

cards.prompt: What problem do idempotency keys solve?
cards.long_answer: When a client retries a request after a network failure, the server may receive the request twice. An idempotency key lets the server detect the duplicate and return the original result instead of performing the action twice.
cards.short_answer: Safely retry without double-applying side effects.
